question_title: In the InfoGripe bulletin published on or immediately before June 25, 2026, which respiratory virus will be the single most prevalent among positive SRAG cases nationally over the most recent 4-week window?
summary: RSV (VSR) is overwhelmingly the most prevalent virus in the InfoGripe 4-week window, confirmed at 49.6% of positive SRAG cases in the most recent bulletin (SE 22, published June 11, 2026). This is nearly double the second-place rhinovirus (24.5%) and more than double influenza A (20.7%). RSV has been trending upward (47.6% → 48.5% → 49.6% over SE 20-22) and is still increasing in most Brazilian regions. The rolling 4-week window creates massive inertia, making it mathematically impossible for RSV to lose its lead in the 1-2 weeks before the target bulletin. Note: the question description contains incorrect figures (rhinovirus 33.2%, VSR 8.5%) that appear to confuse YTD cumulative figures with the 4-week window; the actual bulletin PDF and Fiocruz news page both confirm RSV at 49.6% for the 4-week window, which is the metric specified in the resolution criteria.

factors.description: RSV (VSR) confirmed at 49.6% of positive SRAG cases in the 4-week window (SE 22 bulletin PDF, page 3), nearly double rhinovirus at 24.5%. Verified from the primary resolution source.
RSV trend is upward: 47.6% (SE 20) → 48.5% (SE 21) → 49.6% (SE 22). RSV is still actively rising across most regions.
Rolling 4-week window creates massive inertia. RSV would need to drop from ~50% to below ~25% in 1-2 weeks to lose its lead - mathematically impossible with a rolling average.
Rhinovirus is stable at ~24.5% (second place), predominantly affecting 5-14 year olds. Historically declines as winter deepens.
Influenza A at 20.7% and declining as a share despite absolute cases rising. Would need to more than double to overtake RSV in 1-2 weeks.
All other viruses far too low to contend: Influenza B at 5.7%, SARS-CoV-2 at 2.0%.
factors.supports: Any other virus (Influenza B, SARS-CoV-2, or other)

reasoning:
## Forecast Summary

**Question:** Which respiratory virus will be most prevalent in the InfoGripe bulletin published on or before June 25, 2026?

### Key Finding
The actual SE 22 bulletin (published June 11, 2026) — verified via both the Fiocruz news page and the downloaded PDF — shows **RSV (VSR) at 49.6%** of positive SRAG cases in the 4-week window, far ahead of rhinovirus (24.5%) and influenza A (20.7%).

> ⚠️ **Important discrepancy:** The question description states "rhinovirus 33.2%, VSR 8.5%" but these appear to be incorrect — likely confusing YTD cumulative figures with the 4-week window. The resolution criteria explicitly specify the 4-week window metric.

### Final Probabilities
| Option | Probability |
|--------|------------|
| **VSR (RSV)** | **92%** |
| Influenza A | 4% |
| Rhinovirus | 3% |
| Any other virus | 1% |

### Rationale
- RSV holds a **25+ percentage point lead** over the second-place virus
- RSV is **still trending upward** (47.6% → 48.5% → 49.6% over 3 weeks)
- The **rolling 4-week window** makes it mathematically impossible for RSV to lose its lead in 1-2 bulletins
- Small residual probability accounts for uncertainty about how the question author interprets the data, given the discrepancy in the question description
sources_consulted: [(Metaculus) In the InfoGripe bulletin published on or immediately before June 25, 2026, which respiratory virus will be the single most prevalent among positive SRAG cases nationally over the most recent 4-week window?](https://www.metaculus.com/questions/44052)
[InfoGripe: cresce número de hospitalizações por VSR e influenza A e B](https://agencia.fiocruz.br/infogripe-cresce-numero-de-hospitalizacoes-por-vsr-e-influenza-e-b)
[InfoGripe: Rio de Janeiro mantém curva de crescimento de Covid-19 entre idosos](https://agencia.fiocruz.br/infogripe)
resolution_criteria: This question resolves based on the InfoGripe bulletin published on or immediately before June 25, 2026 (i.e., the most recent bulletin dated on or before that date), as reported on the Agência Fiocruz de Notícias InfoGripe page (https://agencia.fiocruz.br/infogripe). It resolves to the single respiratory virus with the highest reported prevalence (percentage) among laboratory-positive SRAG cases over the most recent four epidemiological weeks ("últimas 4 semanas" / "prevalência entre casos positivos"). Resolution maps the leading virus to one of: "Rhinovirus (Rinovírus)", "Influenza A", "VSR (RSV)", or "Any other virus (Influenza B, SARS-CoV-2, or other)". Example: if rhinovirus is reported at 31% and is the highest of any single virus, this resolves "Rhinovirus (Rinovírus)".
fine_print: If the bulletin reports influenza only as a combined "Influenza (A+B)" figure without a separate Influenza A value, use the Influenza A value if separately reported; the combined figure is not used. "Most recent four epidemiological weeks" refers to the rolling 4-week window the bulletin uses for its "casos positivos" virus distribution, not the cumulative annual figures. If two viruses are tied for the highest prevalence, the question resolves to "Any other virus (Influenza B, SARS-CoV-2, or other)" only if neither tied virus is among the three named options; otherwise it resolves to whichever tied named virus the bulletin lists first. If the InfoGripe page is unavailable, the corresponding bulletin PDF (Resumo_InfoGripe_2026_XX) or reporting by credible sources (https://www.metaculus.com/faq/#definitions) may be used. If no bulletin covering data through at least SE 24 (week ending June 20, 2026) is published on or before June 25, 2026, the most recent available 2026 bulletin as of June 25, 2026 is used.

factors.description: RSV (VSR) is confirmed at 49.6% of positive SRAG cases in the 4-week window (SE 22 bulletin PDF, page 3), nearly double the second-place rhinovirus at 24.5%. This is a massive, confirmed lead from the primary resolution source.
RSV trend is upward: 47.6% (SE 20) → 48.5% (SE 21) → 49.6% (SE 22). RSV is still actively rising, driven by ongoing increases in Nordeste, Sudeste, and Sul regions.

The rolling 4-week window creates enormous inertia. Even if RSV peaked at SE 22, the 4-week average would only decline gradually. To lose its lead, RSV would need to drop from ~50% to below ~25% in 1-2 weeks - mathematically impossible with a rolling average.
Rhinovirus is stable at ~24.5% (second place), predominantly affecting 5-14 year olds. Historically declines as winter deepens. Not a plausible contender for first place.

Influenza A at 20.7% and declining as a SHARE (22.4% → 21.9% → 20.7%) even though absolute cases are rising. Seasonally peaks EW 24-28, but would need to more than double its share to overtake RSV - extremely unlikely in 1-2 weeks.
All other viruses (Influenza B at 5.7%, SARS-CoV-2 at 2.0%) are far too low to be contenders. Combined they're under 8%.
factors.supports: Any other virus (Influenza B, SARS-CoV-2, or other)

input.anchor: RSV at 49.6% in the most recent 4-week window (SE 22), with a strong upward trend and ~25pp lead over second place
input.assessment:
The SE 22 bulletin PDF (the actual resolution source) unambiguously confirms RSV at 49.6% in the 4-week window, nearly double rhinovirus (24.5%) and more than double influenza A (20.7%). 

The question description contains incorrect figures (stating rhinovirus 33.2% and VSR 8.5%), which appear to be either from a different data source, a different time window, or simply erroneous. The resolution criteria explicitly references the "últimas 4 semanas" virus distribution from the InfoGripe bulletin, which I've now verified directly from the PDF.

The target bulletin will be published on or before June 25 - likely SE 23 (around June 18) or SE 24 (around June 25). Given:
1. RSV's 25+ percentage point lead over the second-place virus
2. The 4-week rolling average creating massive inertia
3. RSV's continued upward trend
4. RSV still being reported as increasing in most regions

It is near-certain that RSV will remain the dominant virus in the next bulletin. The only scenario where RSV loses its lead would require an unprecedented, sudden collapse in RSV cases combined with an explosive surge in another virus - something with no historical precedent at this stage of the season.

Influenza A is the main rising virus seasonally and could eventually overtake RSV later in the winter (EW 26-30), but not within the 1-2 week timeframe of this question. Rhinovirus is stable and declining seasonally.
input.calibration_notes: The implied probability from factors (>99% RSV) is even higher than my tentative 92%. I'm keeping some probability mass on alternatives to account for: (1) the possibility that I'm misunderstanding which numbers the resolution will use, given the question description's different figures, (2) an unexpected data revision or correction, (3) the seasonal transition potentially being faster than expected. However, the direct verification from the PDF makes me confident that the 4-week window figures are correct.
input.key_uncertainties:
1. Could the question author's stated figures (rhinovirus 33.2%, VSR 8.5%) reflect a different interpretation of the bulletin that I'm missing? I've verified from the PDF that the 4-week window clearly shows RSV at 49.6%, but the discrepancy is puzzling.
2. Could a new bulletin published before June 25 show dramatically different figures due to data revisions?
input.tool_audit: research() was excellent - retrieved data from 3 consecutive bulletins. fetch_url confirmed the data from the Fiocruz page. The PDF of the SE 22 bulletin was the most critical source, directly confirming the 4-week window figures. The discrepancy with the question description was the main challenge, but primary source verification resolved it clearly.
input.process_reflection: The process worked very well for this question. The research tool pulled comprehensive data, and the ability to download and read the actual PDF was critical for resolving the discrepancy between the question description and the actual data. The main friction was the question description containing what appear to be incorrect figures, which initially caused confusion about which metric the resolution would use.

revision_history.reviewer_assessment:
## Checks Performed

### Hallucinated Evidence
All six factors were verified against the research trace:
- **Factor 1** (RSV at 49.6%): Confirmed by fetch_url of the SE 22 Fiocruz page, which returned "RSV/VSR: 49.6%" for the 4-week window. The PDF was also downloaded and read, with the trace showing the exact Portuguese text: "49,6% de vírus sincicial respiratório."
- **Factor 2** (RSV trend 47.6% → 48.5% → 49.6%): The research tool retrieved data from three consecutive bulletins (SE 20, 21, 22) confirming these exact figures.
- **Factor 4** (Rhinovirus at ~24.5%): Confirmed across all three bulletins in the research output.
- **Factor 5** (Influenza A at 20.7%, declining share 22.4% → 21.9% → 20.7%): Confirmed in the trend analysis table in the research output.
- **Factor 6** (Influenza B 5.7%, SARS-CoV-2 2.0%): Confirmed in the SE 22 bulletin data.

No hallucinated claims found.

### Double-Counting
Factors 1 (current RSV level), 2 (RSV trend), and 3 (rolling-window inertia) all support VSR and draw from the same bulletin data. However, they make distinct arguments: Factor 1 is the current snapshot, Factor 2 is the trajectory, and Factor 3 is a mathematical argument about how rolling averages work. The overlap is mild and doesn't distort the forecast.

### Wrong-Direction Factors
All logit signs are correct. Factors 1-3 are positive for VSR (RSV). Factors 4-6 are negative for their respective categories (Rhinovirus, Influenza A, other viruses). No issues.

### Contradictory Assessment
The assessment is fully consistent with the factors: RSV dominant at ~50%, rhinovirus distant second at ~25%, influenza A third at ~21%, others negligible.

### Resolution Criteria
Resolution criteria were fetched via get_metaculus_questions and are present. The criteria specify: "the single respiratory virus with the highest reported prevalence (percentage) among laboratory-positive SRAG cases over the most recent four epidemiological weeks." The agent correctly engaged with this exact metric from the primary source.

### Resolution Misalignment — Key Discrepancy
The question description states "rhinovirus 33.2%, influenza A 20.5%, VSR 8.5%" for the SE 22 bulletin, but the agent's direct verification of the same bulletin shows RSV at 49.6%, rhinovirus at 24.5%. The agent explicitly addresses this discrepancy, argues the question description is wrong, and provides strong evidence: (1) the Fiocruz news page confirms RSV 49.6%, (2) the downloaded PDF confirms the same, (3) three consecutive bulletins show a consistent RSV-dominant trend. The question's "rhinovirus 33.2%" is close to the YTD cumulative figure (32.5%), suggesting the question author may have confused YTD and 4-week figures. The resolution criteria clearly specify the 4-week window, and the agent verified the correct metric from the primary source.

### Counterargument Quality
The counterargument raises the legitimate concern that the question author might be looking at different data views (e.g., excluding children under 2, or adult-only cases). The agent then dismisses it by noting the resolution criteria explicitly references "prevalência entre casos positivos" which matches the bulletin's reporting. The agent also fetched both the news page and the PDF and got consistent results. The counterargument is adequate — not a token gesture.

### Probability Assessment
With RSV at 49.6% and a 25pp lead, upward trend, and rolling-window inertia (the question resolves on a bulletin published ~10 days from now), 92% for VSR is reasonable. The remaining 8% appropriately accounts for uncertainty about the data discrepancy with the question description and the possibility that a new bulletin could show surprising shifts. I'd estimate 88-92% myself — the agent's 92% is within a reasonable range and not meaningfully different from my independent assessment.

### Tool Audit
Agent claims: "research() was excellent - retrieved data from 3 consecutive bulletins. fetch_url confirmed the data from the Fiocruz page." Ground truth: 1 research call (0 errors), 3 fetch_url calls (0 errors), 2 web_search calls (0 errors), 9 total calls with 0% error rate. The audit is accurate.

### Worldview Consistency
The worldview research entry (infogripe-fiocruz-bulletin-june-2026-respiratory-v-fa5773be) confirms RSV at 49.6% for the 4-week window, consistent with all factors. No contradicting worldview entries found. No worldview forecasts directory exists for cross-question consistency checks.
